hints.tacuerint: 'Tacuerint' is the verb of the protasis (the 'if' clause)
hints.lapides: 'Lapides' is a noun meaning 'stones' — the subject of 'clamabunt'
hints.hi: 'Hi' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'these' — subject of 'tacuerint'

hints.to call: 'To call' is 'vocare' — 'clamabunt' comes from 'clamare' (to cry out)
hints.to claim: 'To claim' is English — 'clamare' means 'to shout/cry out'

steps.reveal: clamabunt = will cry out (3rd pl. future)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'clamant' — 'clamabunt' with -abunt is future
hints.future perfect: Future perfect would be 'clamaverint' — 'clamabunt' is simple future
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'clament' — 'clamabunt' is future indicative

hints.claudo, claudere, clausi, clausum: These are the parts of 'claudo' (to close)
hints.clango, clangere, —, —: These are the parts of 'clango' (to clang/resound)
hints.voco, vocare, vocavi, vocatum: These are the parts of 'voco' (to call)

steps.reveal: Si hi tacuerint = If these will have been silent (fut. perf.)

hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive 'tacuerint' looks identical, but in a future more vivid condition it is future perfect
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'tacuissent' — 'tacuerint' is future perfect
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'tacuerunt' — 'tacuerint' with -int is future perfect

hints.tango, tangere, tetigi, tactum: These are the parts of 'tango' (to touch)
hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'teneo' (to hold)
hints.tollo, tollere, sustuli, sublatum: These are the parts of 'tollo' (to lift/remove)

steps.prompt: What type of conditional is 'Si tacuerint... clamabunt'?
steps.target: future more vivid (si + fut. perf., future indicative)
choices: future more vivid (si + fut. perf., future indicative)
past contrary-to-fact (si + plpf. subj., plpf. subj.)
future less vivid (si + pres. subj., pres. subj.)
steps.reveal: Si + future perfect, future indicative = future more vivid condition

hints.present general (si + pres., pres.): Present general uses present indicative in both clauses — this uses future tenses
hints.past contrary-to-fact (si + plpf. subj.,: Past contrary-to-fact uses pluperfect subjunctive — this uses future tenses
hints.future less vivid (si + pres. subj., pre: Future less vivid uses present subjunctive — this uses future indicative/future perfect

steps.reveal: lapides = the stones (nom. pl., subject of 'clamabunt')

hints.accusative plural: 'Lapides' looks the same in accusative plural, but it is the subject of 'clamabunt'
hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular would be 'lapidis' — 'lapides' is nominative/accusative plural
hints.vocative plural: Vocative 'lapides' looks the same, but stones are not being addressed

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'lapidis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'lapidis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'lapidis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What literary device is at work when stones cry out?

hints.metonymy: Metonymy substitutes a related term — here stones are given the human ability to cry out
hints.synecdoche: Synecdoche uses part for whole — here stones are personified with speech
hints.litotes: Litotes affirms by negating the opposite — this is a vivid personification

translation: If these will have been silent, the stones will cry out
